narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPAs, family income levels, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it estimated a 41% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that triggered the rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The two biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 1 school decile ranking, which placed you lower than most students (around the 2nd decile versus the typical 6th), and your Year 3 decile, which was even lower (1st decile compared to the average 6th), suggesting your grades didn't keep pace with peers who tend to pass the bar. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 35 stood strong, just shy of the average 37, and powerfully pushed the model toward predicting success, as did your lower-middle family income bracket and solid undergraduate GPA of 2.5 (below the 3.2 average but still helpful), along with your full-time study plans that align with the vast majority of successful students. Compared to typical applicants who pass, your school deciles were notably weaker, outweighing those positives and tipping the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your academic record in ways that improve those decile rankings, perhaps through additional coursework or prep programs tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your unique story more heavily. Keep pushing; your journey as a Black woman in law is vital, and with strategic steps, you can turn this around. We're rooting for you.
